original_abstract:
It is widely recognized that training deep neural networks effectively requires massive datasets comprising thousands of annotated samples. In this paper, we present "U-Net," a novel network architecture and training strategy specifically designed to work efficiently with limited data through the heavy use of data augmentation. The architecture features a symmetric design: a contracting path to capture global context and an expanding path that enables precise, pixel-level localization. 

We demonstrate that U-Net can be trained end-to-end using very few images while still outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods—such as sliding-window convolutional networks—on the ISBI challenge for segmenting neuronal structures in electron microscopy stacks. Furthermore, our network achieved a decisive victory in the 2015 ISBI Cell Tracking Challenge for transmitted light microscopy categories (phase contrast and DIC) by a significant margin. Beyond its accuracy, the network is highly efficient; a single 512x512 image can be segmented in less than a second on a modern GPU. We have made our full Caffe-based implementation and pre-trained networks available at: http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net.
humanized_abstract:
Deep learning is famously about training extremely large number of parameters in the model. Thus to train deep learning models, we really need a ton of training data. We talking about thousands of images or samples with ground truth annotations (which is an exhaustive set of labels for all samples). Here, we talk about U-Net as one possible deep learning network that can be adapted and learn to quickly make best use of relatively small number of samples thanks to highly increasing impact of using a high number of different augmentations. In other words: the architecture defined in this paper is focused on being as effective as possible when only small amounts of data are available. They make heavy use of data augmentation, while defining an architecture composed by a contracting path (in the downhill arrow) and an expansive path (in the uphill arrow).

This paper demonstrates that the U-Net can be learned from very few examples and that it achieves state-of-the-art results in the ISBI challenge for neuronal segmentation from electron microscopy stacks. The method outperforms recent sliding-window approaches. In addition, we submitted the U-Net to the 2015 ISBI Cell Tracking Challenge for the transmitted light microscopy categories phase contrast and DIC and won first place with a clear margin. The network is very fast. Segmentation of a single 512x512 image takes less than a second on a modern GPU. Our full Caffe-based implementation and the pre-trained models for the 3D neuronal segmentation challenge are available from: http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net.

document.output:
Deep learning is famously about training extremely large number of parameters in the model. Thus to train deep learning models, we really need a ton of training data. We talking about thousands of images or samples with ground truth annotations (which is an exhaustive set of labels for all samples). Here, we talk about U-Net as one possible deep learning network that can be adapted and learn to quickly make best use of relatively small number of samples thanks to highly increasing impact of using a high number of different augmentations. In other words: the architecture defined in this paper is focused on being as effective as possible when only small amounts of data are available. They make heavy use of data augmentation, while defining an architecture composed by a contracting path (in the downhill arrow) and an expansive path (in the uphill arrow).

This paper demonstrates that the U-Net can be learned from very few examples and that it achieves state-of-the-art results in the ISBI challenge for neuronal segmentation from electron microscopy stacks. The method outperforms recent sliding-window approaches. In addition, we submitted the U-Net to the 2015 ISBI Cell Tracking Challenge for the transmitted light microscopy categories phase contrast and DIC and won first place with a clear margin. The network is very fast. Segmentation of a single 512x512 image takes less than a second on a modern GPU. Our full Caffe-based implementation and the pre-trained models for the 3D neuronal segmentation challenge are available from: http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net.
